solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
1571G - A Battle Against a Dragon
Obviously, the problem requires some sort of dynamic programming.
Consider the simplest one. $$$dp_{i,j}$$$ is the maximum total damage dealt by the first $$$i$$$ warriors if there are $$$j$$$ barricades after the move of the $$$i$$$-th warrior. The transitions are exactly the same as they are written in the statement.
That solution works in $$$O(nm)$$$ which is too slow. Let's analyze the behavior of the transitions on the dp table. The transition that destroys a barricade moves the value in some cell $$$(i,j)$$$ to $$$(i+1,j-1)$$$. The transition, that does nothing, moves it to $$$(i+1,j)$$$. The important thing to notice is that only the attack transition changes the value before updating the other state.
Thus, the idea is to write the dp in such a way that useless states produced by the first two transitions aren't stored explicitly and only collected by the attack transitions.
Let's restate the dp. $$$dp_{i,k}$$$ is the total damage dealt by the first $$$i$$$ warriors if the $$$i$$$-th warrior used his $$$k$$$-th attack. Notice that the fact that the attack was possible helps to identify the current number of barricades. To update such a dp we should iterate over both the previous warrior to attack and the attack he used.
Let the previous warrior be $$$j (j  \lt  i)$$$, his attack was when there were $$$x$$$ barricades and the attack of the $$$i$$$-th warrior was when there were $$$y$$$ barricades. If $$$x  \lt  y$$$, then there was no way for the $$$i$$$-th warrior to use his attack. At the same time, if $$$x - y \ge i - j$$$, then the warriors between the $$$j$$$-th and the $$$i$$$-th one have no way to destroy enough barricades. Otherwise, there is a way to assign either of the two moves to them in such a way that exactly $$$y$$$ barricades are left. Thus, $$$y \le x \le (i - j) + y$$$ should hold for the transition to be possible.
Picture a set of possible states, which can update $$$dp_{i,k}$$$, on the table. It looks like a triangle starting from $$$(i-1,y)$$$ and going vertically to the top and diagonally to the top-right.
That screams data structures, and we are finally approaching them.
I propose the following way to calculate the dp. Sort the attacks of all warriors altogether, first, in the decreasing order of the number of barricades and, second, in the increasing order of the warrior index.
Store a BIT such that its $$$i$$$-th position contains the maximum value of dp over the $$$i$$$-th diagonal (going from top-right to bottom-left). Now you can forget that the query area is a triangle and query on the entire prefix of the diagonals. The values in the required area are calculated already, and the values outside of it that get captured by the query are not calculated yet (due to being to the left of the triangle).
The answer is the maximum value stored in the entire BIT at the end.
Overall complexity: $$$O((\sum \limits_{i=1}^n k_i) \cdot \log(n + m))$$$.
